subject: Constitutional Law
module_title: International Relations and European Union Law
lesson_title: The European Convention on Human Rights in Irish Law

questions.question: Before the European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003, the status of the ECHR in Irish domestic law was established in Re Ó Laighléis [1960] IR 93, where the Supreme Court held that:

options.A: The ECHR was automatically part of Irish domestic law as a ratified treaty
options.B: The ECHR, though ratified by Ireland, was not part of Irish domestic law and could not be relied upon to confer enforceable rights in Irish courts — Ireland's dualist system required incorporation by legislation
options.C: The ECHR had constitutional force equivalent to the fundamental rights provisions in the Constitution
options.D: Irish courts were required to interpret domestic law in light of the ECHR even without legislative incorporation

questions.explanation: Re Ó Laighléis [1960] IR 93 is the foundational authority establishing that Ireland follows a dualist approach to international law. The Supreme Court held that the ECHR, while ratified by Ireland in 1953, had not been incorporated into domestic law by the Oireachtas and therefore could not be relied upon as a source of enforceable rights in Irish courts. The plaintiff sought to rely on ECHR rights in a habeas corpus application but the Court rejected this, holding that unincorporated treaty obligations are not part of Irish domestic law. This position remained the law until the European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003.

questions.question: The European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003 incorporated the ECHR into Irish law. The status accorded to the ECHR by the 2003 Act is:

options.A: Constitutional status — the ECHR prevails over all inconsistent legislation and constitutional provisions
options.B: Sub-constitutional statutory status — the ECHR is part of Irish domestic law but is subordinate to the Constitution; the Act does not amend the Constitution and Convention rights cannot override constitutional provisions
options.C: Equivalent to EU law — the ECHR has direct effect and primacy over all domestic law
options.D: Purely aspirational — the Act creates no enforceable rights and merely guides statutory interpretation

questions.explanation: The ECHR Act 2003 gives the Convention a sub-constitutional status in Irish law. The Act incorporates ECHR rights into domestic law but explicitly does not amend the Constitution. The Preamble and structure of the Act make clear that the ECHR operates at the level of statute: it prevails over (or requires interpretation of) ordinary legislation, but is subordinate to the Constitution. This means that where ECHR rights conflict with the Constitution, the Constitution prevails. This sub-constitutional model mirrors the UK's Human Rights Act 1998, though Ireland's constitutional rights guarantees often provide equivalent or stronger protection than the ECHR.

questions.question: Section 3(1) of the European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003 imposes an obligation on 'organs of the State' to perform their functions in a manner compatible with the Convention. An 'organ of the State' for the purposes of the Act includes:

options.A: Only central Government departments and An Garda Síochána
options.B: Every person or body whose powers or functions are derived from or founded on Irish law, including courts, tribunals, public bodies, local authorities, and State agencies — but not purely private parties
options.C: All persons in Ireland, including private individuals and companies
options.D: Only bodies established by statute — not bodies established under common law or prerogative

questions.explanation: Section 3(1) of the ECHR Act 2003 provides that every organ of the State shall perform its functions in a manner compatible with the State's obligations under the Convention. 'Organ of the State' is broadly defined to include courts, tribunals, bodies established by legislation, and all public authorities performing public functions. Private individuals and purely private companies are not directly bound by the Convention obligation under s.3(1). However, because courts are themselves organs of the State, their application of common law and statute to private disputes must itself be Convention-compatible — creating an indirect horizontal effect in some circumstances.

questions.question: Section 2(1) of the ECHR Act 2003 provides that courts shall, 'in so far as is possible, subject to the rules of law relating to such interpretation and application,' interpret and apply statutory provisions in a manner compatible with the State's obligations under the Convention. This interpretive obligation:

options.A: Requires courts to disapply statutory provisions that are incompatible with the ECHR
options.B: Requires courts to give Convention-compatible interpretations to statutory provisions where possible, but does not empower courts to override or disapply unambiguous legislation that is incompatible with the Convention
options.C: Applies only to legislation enacted after 2003
options.D: Creates a duty to read all domestic law in light of Strasbourg case law, even where domestic constitutional rights provide greater protection

questions.explanation: Section 2(1) of the ECHR Act 2003 creates an interpretive obligation — courts must, where possible, interpret statutory provisions in a Convention-compatible manner. However, this is a rule of interpretation, not a power to override legislation. Where a statutory provision is unambiguously incompatible with the Convention and cannot be read compatibly, the court cannot disapply it (unlike EU law under Art. 29.4.6°). Instead, the appropriate remedy is a declaration of incompatibility under s.5. This mirrors the approach under the UK Human Rights Act 1998, s.3. The phrase 'in so far as is possible' indicates that the interpretive obligation is strong but not unlimited — it cannot lead to an interpretation that contradicts the clear and unambiguous meaning of the statutory text.

questions.question: Section 5 of the ECHR Act 2003 empowers the High Court and Supreme Court to make a 'declaration of incompatibility' where a statutory provision cannot be interpreted compatibly with the Convention. A declaration of incompatibility:

options.A: Automatically invalidates the incompatible statutory provision — it ceases to have legal effect from the date of the declaration
options.B: Does not affect the validity or continuing application of the statutory provision — it is a statement that the provision is incompatible with the Convention, which may prompt legislative action but does not strike down the law
options.C: Creates a mandatory obligation on the Oireachtas to amend the incompatible provision within six months
options.D: Entitles the plaintiff to damages from the State as of right upon the making of the declaration

questions.explanation: A declaration of incompatibility under s.5 of the ECHR Act 2003 is a declaratory remedy only. It does not invalidate the statutory provision or affect its legal operation — the provision remains in force unless and until the Oireachtas amends or repeals it. This contrasts sharply with a declaration of invalidity under Art. 26 or Art. 34 of the Constitution, which does render a law void. The distinction reflects the sub-constitutional status of the ECHR: courts cannot strike down primary legislation on Convention grounds as they can on constitutional grounds. A declaration of incompatibility may give rise to an ex gratia payment or legislative reform, but there is no automatic right to damages or mandatory obligation on the Oireachtas to act.

questions.question: The key distinction between a declaration of incompatibility under s.5 of the ECHR Act 2003 and a declaration of invalidity under the Constitution is:

options.A: There is no practical distinction — both remedies result in the law being unenforceable
options.B: A declaration of invalidity renders the offending statutory provision void and of no legal effect (under constitutional supremacy); a declaration of incompatibility leaves the statutory provision in force and merely signals its incompatibility with the Convention — the Oireachtas is not legally obliged to act
options.C: Declarations of incompatibility can only be made by the Supreme Court; declarations of invalidity can be made by any court
options.D: A declaration of incompatibility attracts mandatory compensation; a declaration of invalidity does not

questions.explanation: This distinction is fundamental to understanding the ECHR Act 2003's sub-constitutional model. A declaration of constitutional invalidity (under the Constitution, s.34) renders a law void — it is struck down and cannot be applied. This flows from the Constitution's supremacy as the fundamental law. By contrast, a declaration of incompatibility under s.5 ECHR Act 2003 merely declares that a provision is incompatible with Convention rights — it does not affect its validity or enforceability. The Oireachtas may respond by amending the law, but is not legally required to do so. This design reflects the Oireachtas's legislative sovereignty and the Convention's sub-constitutional status.

questions.question: The question of whether the ECHR Act 2003 creates 'horizontal' effect — binding purely private parties — was considered in Zappone and Gilligan v Revenue Commissioners [2008] 2 IR 417. The court's approach to horizontal effect under the Act was:

options.A: Full horizontal direct effect — the ECHR binds all private parties in Ireland as if it were constitutional law
options.B: No horizontal effect whatsoever — the ECHR Act 2003 has no application to disputes between private parties
options.C: Indirect horizontal effect — since courts are organs of the State bound by s.3(1), they must act in a Convention-compatible manner when adjudicating private disputes, which may indirectly influence the outcome; but Convention rights do not directly bind private parties
options.D: Horizontal effect limited to employment relationships only

questions.explanation: The ECHR Act 2003 does not create full horizontal direct effect: Convention rights do not directly bind private parties in the way they bind organs of the State under s.3(1). However, because courts are themselves organs of the State under s.3(1), they must apply and develop the law (including common law rules governing private disputes) in a manner compatible with Convention obligations. This creates indirect horizontal effect — where a court's application of legal rules to a private dispute would, if applied in a Convention-incompatible way, make the court itself act incompatibly with s.3(1). The approach is similar to the indirect horizontal effect recognised in the UK under the Human Rights Act 1998.

questions.question: In McD v L and Anor [2010] IESC 51, the Supreme Court considered the interaction between the constitutional right of a natural father and the ECHR right to family life under Art. 8. The Court held:

options.A: That the ECHR right to family life under Art. 8 prevailed over the constitutional position on the rights of a natural father
options.B: That the Irish Constitution provided the applicable framework, and the ECHR Act 2003 (sub-constitutional in status) could not override or modify constitutional rights — where Convention rights and constitutional rights diverge, the Constitution prevails
options.C: That there was no conflict between Art. 8 ECHR and the Constitution in this case
options.D: That the ECHR Act 2003 required the court to follow the European Court of Human Rights jurisprudence in all cases involving family rights

questions.explanation: McD v L and Anor [2010] IESC 51 is an important authority on the hierarchy of rights under the ECHR Act 2003. The Supreme Court confirmed that the ECHR operates at a sub-constitutional level — the Act does not amend the Constitution and cannot override constitutional provisions. Where ECHR rights (such as Art. 8's right to family life, which the Strasbourg court interpreted in a particular way regarding unmarried fathers) diverge from the constitutional position (which gives limited rights to natural fathers compared to those within the marital family), the Constitution prevails. The court is not bound to follow ECtHR jurisprudence that would conflict with constitutional provisions.

questions.question: Under s.4 of the ECHR Act 2003, Irish courts must 'take due account' of the principles laid down by the European Court of Human Rights when interpreting and applying Convention provisions. 'Taking due account' means:

options.A: Irish courts are absolutely bound by all ECtHR judgments and must follow them as binding precedent in all cases
options.B: Irish courts must consider ECtHR judgments and give them significant weight as persuasive authority, but are not absolutely bound to follow them — they retain discretion, particularly where following Strasbourg jurisprudence would conflict with the Constitution or established Irish law
options.C: Irish courts are not required to consider ECtHR judgments at all — s.4 is purely aspirational
options.D: Irish courts must follow ECtHR judgments unless the Oireachtas has specifically legislated to the contrary

questions.explanation: Section 4 of the ECHR Act 2003 requires courts to 'take due account of' principles laid down by the ECtHR — a formula that is less than absolute binding precedent but more than merely optional consideration. It imposes a duty to engage with Strasbourg jurisprudence and give it significant weight. However, the phrase 'take due account' does not create a rule of stare decisis equivalent to precedent in the domestic courts. Courts retain discretion to depart from ECtHR jurisprudence, particularly where following it would conflict with a constitutional provision or where there are sound reasons to prefer an alternative interpretation. The standard is closer to 'strongly persuasive authority' than to binding precedent.

questions.question: A person claims that An Garda Síochána, in conducting a search of their home, violated their right to privacy under Art. 8 ECHR. Under the ECHR Act 2003, the available remedies in an Irish court include:

options.A: Only damages — no other remedy is available under the Act
options.B: No remedy — only the European Court of Human Rights in Strasbourg can award remedies for ECHR violations
options.C: Such remedy as the court considers appropriate, which may include damages (s.3(2)), an injunction, a declaration, or other equitable or legal relief — the Act gives courts a broad remedial discretion for breaches of the s.3(1) obligation by organs of the State
options.D: A mandatory declaration of incompatibility, which automatically invalidates the legislation authorising the search

questions.explanation: Section 3(2) of the ECHR Act 2003 provides that a person who suffers injury, loss, or damage as a result of a contravention of s.3(1) by an organ of the State may, if no other remedy in damages is available, apply to the appropriate court for damages. The courts have broad remedial discretion to award 'such remedy as it considers appropriate' including damages, injunctions, and declaratory relief. Crucially, damages are available only where no other adequate remedy exists — the Act gives domestic courts the power to provide effective remedies for Convention violations without requiring every applicant to go to Strasbourg. An applicant must exhaust domestic remedies (including those under the Act) before applying to the ECtHR.
